---
title: "Day 5 — Exercise: `--release` is a 50× free lunch"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Measure how much faster a Rust program runs when compiled with `cargo build --release` (or `cargo run --release`) compared to the default debug profile. This is the single most important Rust-specific flag for bioinformatics — debug code is slow, release code is competitive with C.

There is no function to write today. The program is provided and computes a real workload (counting all k-mers across many random sequences); your job is to **measure**.

## Background

`cargo build` defaults to `--debug`: minimal optimisations, bounds-check assertions on, fast to compile, slow to run. The output goes to `target/debug/`. This is what `cargo test` and `cargo run` use unless you say otherwise.

`cargo build --release` enables `opt-level = 3` (full LLVM optimisations: vectorisation, inlining, loop unrolling), strips overflow checks, and produces an output in `target/release/`. Slow to compile, fast to run. On the workloads in this course, expect 10×–100× speedup over debug.

Students who skip this flag and then complain about "Rust being slow" haunt the language's reputation. Don't be that student.

## The exercise

```bash
cd day5/ex-release-mode
```

The program counts every 7-mer across `N` random sequences of length 1000 and reports the running time. No coding required — just run it both ways and write down the numbers.

## How to run

```bash
# Debug build:
time cargo run -- 1000

# Release build:
time cargo run --release -- 1000
```

Both forms also print their own elapsed time, measured with [`std::time::Instant`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/time/struct.Instant.html), so you don't need a separate stopwatch.

Increase the count (e.g. `1000` → `5000`) until the release build takes around 1 second. The debug build will take **much** longer for the same workload.

## What to observe

1. **Release speedup factor.** On a modern laptop you should see at least 20×. Speedups of 50× are normal for tight loops over byte slices.
2. **Compile time.** The first release build is much slower than the first debug build, because LLVM is doing real optimisation work. After that, incremental compiles are similar.
3. **Output size.** `ls -lah target/debug/ex-release-mode target/release/ex-release-mode` — debug binaries are usually larger because they include debug info; release binaries are smaller and faster.

## What you do NOT need to do

- Add the `#[bench]` attribute or use any benchmarking framework today. The simple `Instant::now() ... elapsed()` pattern in the provided code is the right tool for "show me the order of magnitude". For statistically rigorous benchmarks, [`criterion`](https://docs.rs/criterion/) is the standard choice — but we will not need it.

## Where this leads

- The same `--release` flag underlies every real Rust bioinformatics tool you have heard of. When you publish your own, distribute the release binary.
- The other knob worth knowing about: [link-time optimisation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/profiles.html#lto) (LTO). Add `lto = "thin"` to your `[profile.release]` for a few extra percent of speed at the cost of more compile time.
- **Exercise 03**: parallelise the same workload across CPU cores with [`rayon`](https://docs.rs/rayon/).
